specialist_failures.reason: non-JSON: JSON parse failed even after repair: Expected ',' or '}' after property value in JSON at position 16910 (line 229 column 198)

accepted_findings.source_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term
accepted_findings.proposed_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3 or Lattice's failure to meet the service-level commitments set forth in Exhibit A for three (3) consecutive months, Customer shall pay all Fees accrued through the effective date of termination and no further Fees thereafter
accepted_findings.external_comment: The current provision requires full remainder-of-term fees even when Customer terminates for non-breach reasons such as repeated service-level failures not rising to material breach. This creates economic lock-in inconsistent with the voluntary nature of SaaS subscriptions. We propose that early termination fees apply only when Customer terminates without cause, and that repeated SLA failures constitute sufficient cause to exit without penalty.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Under the current language, if Customer signs a three-year Initial Term and decides after 18 months that the service no longer meets needs due to repeated performance issues, Customer owes 18 months of additional fees despite receiving no further services. This is functionally a liquidated-damages provision that may not survive enforceability scrutiny, and it strips Customer of flexibility to respond to inadequate performance. Repeated SLA failures may cripple operations without rising to 'material breach' if Lattice cures within the 60-day window each time.
accepted_findings.position: Early termination fees waived if Customer terminates for repeated SLA failures (defined as three consecutive months below SLA targets) or other cause short of breach. In the absence of cause, Customer pays a reasonable early-termination fee capped at three months of fees or 25% of the remaining term, whichever is less.
accepted_findings.fallback: Early termination fee capped at six months of fees or 50% of the remaining term, whichever is less, with no fee if termination follows three consecutive months of SLA failures.
accepted_findings.walkaway: Full remainder-of-term fees due regardless of cause for early termination
accepted_findings.suggested_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3 or Lattice's failure to meet the service-level commitments set forth in Exhibit A for three (3) consecutive months, Customer shall pay all Fees accrued through the effective date of termination and no further Fees thereafter

accepted_findings.category: termination for breach — asymmetric cure periods
accepted_findings.source_text: Lattice may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to Customer if Customer materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail. Customer may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to Lattice if Lattice materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within sixty (60) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to the other Party if such other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The current provision grants Lattice twice the time to remedy a material breach while Customer faces termination at 31 days. In a SaaS context where vendor breaches such as extended downtime or data-security failures may cause immediate operational harm, mutual cure periods are a core principle of balanced risk allocation. We request symmetric 30-day cure periods for both parties.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Asymmetric cure periods give Lattice 60 days to remedy material breaches while Customer has only 30 days. This denies Customer the reciprocal early-exit right for vendor performance failures that cause immediate business harm. The asymmetry directly contravenes the requirement for parity on cure periods in commercial agreements of this nature.
accepted_findings.position: Mutual 30-day cure periods
accepted_findings.fallback: Mutual 45-day cure periods with a right for either Party to terminate immediately in the event of a breach that causes imminent harm or cannot be cured
accepted_findings.walkaway: Asymmetric cure periods where Customer has materially shorter cure time than Lattice
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to the other Party if such other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Add new Section 9.1(a): 'Subcontractor Obligations. Lattice shall require all subcontractors that process Customer Data or provide material components of the Subscription Services to comply with obligations no less protective than those imposed on Lattice under this Agreement, including without limitation the confidentiality obligations in Section 7, the data security obligations in Section 5.2, and the insurance requirements in Section 9.2. Lattice shall remain fully liable for the acts and omissions of its subcontractors as if they were Lattice's own acts and omissions. Upon Customer's written request, Lattice shall provide Customer with a list of material subcontractors (defined as subcontractors processing Customer Data or providing more than 25% of the Subscription Services functionality) and shall provide Customer with thirty (30) days' prior written notice of any change to such material subcontractors, during which period Customer may object on reasonable grounds related to data security, compliance, or confidentiality. If Customer objection cannot be resolved within fifteen (15) days, Customer may terminate the affected Subscription Service without penalty.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: Section 9.1 permits Lattice to engage third parties to perform obligations including processing Customer Data, but does not require contractual flowdown of security, confidentiality, and insurance obligations to subcontractors, nor does it provide Customer with notice or approval rights for subcontractors handling Customer Data. In a SaaS context where subcontractors routinely have direct access to Customer Data, this creates compliance and security risk. We request adding flowdown obligations, notice and approval rights for material subcontractors, and termination rights if objections cannot be resolved.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without flowdown obligations, Lattice may remain 'responsible' for subcontractor performance but lack contractual leverage to enforce that responsibility. Without notice or approval rights, Customer has no visibility into which third parties hold Customer Data and cannot vet them for security or compliance. In agreements processing sensitive operational data, lack of subcontractor governance creates significant compliance gaps under data-breach notification laws and industry-specific regulations.
accepted_findings.position: Notice required + Customer approval for material subcontractors (defined as those processing Customer Data or providing >25% of services). Vendor must impose flowdown of confidentiality, data-security, and insurance obligations on all subcontractors. Customer may object to subcontractor changes on reasonable data-security or compliance grounds, with termination right if objection cannot be resolved.
accepted_findings.fallback: Notice required for material subcontractors with 15-day objection window; no approval required but Customer may terminate without penalty if new subcontractor creates material compliance or security risk. Flowdown obligations required.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per §10.1). Subcontracting without flowdown obligations or customer notice is common in vendor-favorable SaaS agreements but creates compliance and security risk for customers in regulated industries or handling sensitive data.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Add new Section 9.1(a): 'Subcontractor Obligations. Lattice shall require all subcontractors that process Customer Data or provide material components of the Subscription Services to comply with obligations no less protective than those imposed on Lattice under this Agreement, including without limitation the confidentiality obligations in Section 7, the data security obligations in Section 5.2, and the insurance requirements in Section 9.2. Lattice shall remain fully liable for the acts and omissions of its subcontractors as if they were Lattice's own acts and omissions. Upon Customer's written request, Lattice shall provide Customer with a list of material subcontractors (defined as subcontractors processing Customer Data or providing more than 25% of the Subscription Services functionality) and shall provide Customer with thirty (30) days' prior written notice of any change to such material subcontractors, during which period Customer may object on reasonable grounds related to data security, compliance, or confidentiality. If Customer objection cannot be resolved within fifteen (15) days, Customer may terminate the affected Subscription Service without penalty.'

accepted_findings.source_text: §3.4: 'If any amount owing by Customer is more than thirty (30) days overdue, Lattice may, without limiting its other rights and remedies, suspend Customer's access to the Subscription Services until such amounts are paid in full. Lattice will provide Customer with at least ten (10) days' prior notice that Customer's account is overdue before suspending the Subscription Services.' Cross-referenced with §4.3: Customer breach termination requires 30-day cure; Vendor breach termination requires 60-day cure.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We note an asymmetry in remedy timelines: Lattice may suspend services for nonpayment after a 40-day total window (30 days overdue + 10 days' notice), while Customer must provide Lattice with a 60-day cure period for material breach before terminating. We propose aligning these timelines so that suspension for nonpayment is permitted only after the same cure period Customer must afford Lattice for other material breaches, or alternatively reducing Lattice's breach-cure period to match the payment-default timeline.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: The interaction creates a leverage imbalance: Vendor may suspend critical services for payment delays that may be administrative or disputed on a 40-day timeline, while Customer must wait 60+ days to terminate for Vendor's material service failures. This is particularly acute where service suspension itself causes business harm exceeding the disputed invoice amount, giving Vendor disproportionate remedy-speed advantage.
accepted_findings.position: Align suspension timeline with breach-cure parity: either (a) suspension for nonpayment permitted only after 60 days overdue with 10-day notice, matching Vendor's breach-cure period, or (b) reduce Vendor's breach-cure period in §4.3 to 30 days to match payment-default remedies.
accepted_findings.fallback: Suspension for nonpayment permitted after 45 days overdue with 15-day notice; disputed amounts under §3.6 (if added) exempt from suspension during resolution period.
accepted_findings.walkaway: Suspension timeline must not be shorter than Customer's termination-cure timeline for Vendor breach; cannot accept 40-day suspension vs 60-day cure asymmetry on agreements exceeding $100K annually.

accepted_findings.source_text: The arbitration will be conducted in San Francisco, California by a single arbitrator selected in accordance with such rules.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: The arbitration will be conducted in a location mutually agreed by the Parties, or, in the absence of agreement, in the city where Customer's principal place of business is located, by a single arbitrator selected in accordance with such rules.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Exclusive arbitration in San Francisco imposes travel, lodging, and local-counsel coordination costs on Customer in any dispute. Standard practice for agreements of this nature is to provide for arbitration in a mutually agreed neutral location or in the customer's home jurisdiction.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Mandatory arbitration in San Francisco creates concrete cost disadvantage for Customer: airfare, lodging, local counsel retention, and employee time away from operations. For disputes under the liability cap (likely $100K–$500K for a mid-market SaaS deal), the cost of prosecuting or defending in San Francisco may approach or exceed the amount in controversy, effectively denying Customer economic remedies for smaller claims.
accepted_findings.position: Arbitration in Customer's home state or mutually agreed neutral location
accepted_findings.fallback: Arbitration in the state where the respondent is headquartered (floating venue)
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware governing law (Section 10.1), but arbitration situs is California per Section 10.2
accepted_findings.suggested_text: The arbitration will be conducted in a location mutually agreed by the Parties, or, in the absence of agreement, in the city where Customer's principal place of business is located, by a single arbitrator selected in accordance with such rules.

accepted_findings.source_text: THE PARTIES KNOWINGLY AND VOLUNTARILY WAIVE ANY RIGHT TO A TRIAL BY JURY AND ANY RIGHT TO PARTICIPATE IN A CLASS, COLLECTIVE, OR REPRESENTATIVE ACTION.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Section 10.2 stacks three dispute-resolution restrictions: mandatory binding arbitration with no appeal, jury waiver, and class-action waiver. In combination, these provisions eliminate Customer's access to multiple traditional remedies and concentrate significant procedural advantage with the party better positioned to fund arbitration. We request that the class-action waiver be removed, or in the alternative, that the arbitration clause permit Customer to elect court litigation for claims under $50,000.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: In combination, these waivers eliminate nearly all low-cost dispute-resolution paths for Customer. For claims below the liability cap (likely $100K–$300K), the cost of AAA arbitration in San Francisco may approach or exceed the recovery, effectively denying Customer remedies for smaller breaches. Class-action waivers prevent aggregation with other customers experiencing similar harm, which is critical in SaaS contexts where widespread defects affect many customers but cause individually small damages.
accepted_findings.position: Remove class-action waiver; permit Customer to elect court litigation for claims under $50,000; or provide for streamlined arbitration for claims under $25,000.
accepted_findings.fallback: Class-action waiver remains, but Customer may elect court litigation for claims under $25,000, and arbitration costs above $10,000 are split 50/50 or borne by Lattice for claims where Customer prevails.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware governing law (Section 10.1); enforceability of stacked waivers varies by jurisdiction but generally upheld in Delaware for commercial parties

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Add new subsection (D) to Section 8.6: '(D) EITHER PARTY'S LIABILITY FOR A SECURITY INCIDENT (AS DEFINED IN SECTION 5.3), WHICH SHALL BE CAPPED AT THE LESSER OF (I) THREE TIMES (3X) THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE SECURITY INCIDENT, OR (II) THE LIMITS OF THE RESPONSIBLE PARTY'S APPLICABLE CYBER LIABILITY INSURANCE COVERAGE.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: Customer requires a dedicated super-cap for Security Incidents involving Customer Data. Given the nature of the data processed and regulatory exposure for data breaches, the standard liability cap of 1x fees does not provide adequate recourse. A 3x super-cap or alignment with cyber insurance limits is standard for SaaS agreements of this type and appropriately balances the parties' interests.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without a data-breach super-cap, Customer's maximum recovery for a Security Incident affecting thousands of records is capped at 1x fees (potentially $100K-$500K in a mid-market deal), whereas Customer's regulatory penalties, notification costs, credit monitoring obligations, and litigation exposure routinely exceed this amount by multiples in material breaches. This creates uninsured exposure for Customer.
accepted_findings.position: Security Incidents carved out from the liability cap with a super-cap at 3x trailing fees or cyber insurance limits, whichever is less.
accepted_findings.fallback: Security Incidents carved out with a super-cap at 2x trailing fees.
accepted_findings.walkaway: Security Incidents subject only to the general 1x cap with no enhancement.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per Section 10.1). Data-breach liability exposure analysis assumes U.S. state-level data-breach notification statutes apply to Customer.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Add new subsection (D) to Section 8.6: '(D) EITHER PARTY'S LIABILITY FOR A SECURITY INCIDENT (AS DEFINED IN SECTION 5.3), WHICH SHALL BE CAPPED AT THE LESSER OF (I) THREE TIMES (3X) THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE SECURITY INCIDENT, OR (II) THE LIMITS OF THE RESPONSIBLE PARTY'S APPLICABLE CYBER LIABILITY INSURANCE COVERAGE.'

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Add new subsection (E) to Section 8.6: '(E) EITHER PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE, WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, OR FRAUD.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: Customer requests a carve-out for gross negligence, willful misconduct, and fraud, which is standard in commercial agreements of this type. Capping liability for deliberate or reckless conduct is contrary to public policy in most jurisdictions and removes meaningful deterrence for egregious conduct. This carve-out applies symmetrically to both parties.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without this carve-out, Lattice's liability for gross negligence or willful misconduct (e.g., intentional deletion of Customer Data, deliberate circumvention of security controls, fraudulent misrepresentation of compliance) is capped at 1x fees. This effectively immunizes Lattice from consequences of egregious conduct and denies Customer proportionate recourse for intentional or reckless harm.
accepted_findings.position: Gross negligence, willful misconduct, and fraud carved out entirely from the liability cap (uncapped).
accepted_findings.fallback: Gross negligence and willful misconduct subject to a 3x super-cap; fraud uncapped.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Many states void contractual limits on liability for gross negligence or willful misconduct as against public policy, making this carve-out both legally prudent and commercially standard.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Add new subsection (E) to Section 8.6: '(E) EITHER PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE, WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, OR FRAUD.'

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Add new Section 8.9: 'Customer IP and Confidentiality Indemnity. Customer will defend Lattice against any claim, suit, or proceeding brought by a third party alleging that (a) Customer Data, when used by Lattice in accordance with this Agreement, infringes such third party's intellectual property rights, or (b) Customer's breach of Section 7 (Confidentiality) with respect to Lattice Confidential Information has caused harm to such third party, and will indemnify Lattice for any damages finally awarded against Lattice (or amounts paid in settlement approved by Customer) in connection with any such claim. The indemnification procedure set forth in Section 8.5 shall apply to Customer's obligations under this Section 8.9.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: Customer proposes mutual indemnification for IP infringement and confidentiality breaches. Section 8.3 provides Lattice IP indemnity to Customer; reciprocal indemnity from Customer for third-party claims arising from Customer Data or Customer's confidentiality breach creates symmetry and reflects industry standard practice.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: The current draft imposes IP and confidentiality indemnity only on Lattice, with no reciprocal obligation from Customer despite Customer's control over Customer Data and access to Lattice Confidential Information. This asymmetry shifts uncompensated risk to Lattice for third-party claims arising from Customer's IP or confidentiality violations. Profile requires symmetry on indemnities of equal weight.
accepted_findings.position: Mutual indemnity for IP infringement and confidentiality breach, with symmetric scope and procedure.
accepted_findings.fallback: Customer indemnity limited to third-party claims arising from Customer Data only (no confidentiality breach indemnity), with 'sole and exclusive remedy' language removed from Section 8.3 to preserve common-law contribution rights.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Indemnity provisions are enforceable under Delaware law subject to clear and unambiguous language; mutuality does not alter enforceability.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Add new Section 8.9: 'Customer IP and Confidentiality Indemnity. Customer will defend Lattice against any claim, suit, or proceeding brought by a third party alleging that (a) Customer Data, when used by Lattice in accordance with this Agreement, infringes such third party's intellectual property rights, or (b) Customer's breach of Section 7 (Confidentiality) with respect to Lattice Confidential Information has caused harm to such third party, and will indemnify Lattice for any damages finally awarded against Lattice (or amounts paid in settlement approved by Customer) in connection with any such claim. The indemnification procedure set forth in Section 8.5 shall apply to Customer's obligations under this Section 8.9.'

accepted_findings.source_text: unless either Party provides written notice of non-renewal to the other Party at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term
accepted_findings.proposed_text: unless either Party provides written notice of non-renewal to the other Party at least thirty (30) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term
accepted_findings.external_comment: A 60-day non-renewal notice period significantly exceeds the standard for SaaS subscriptions of this type and creates operational friction. We request a 30-day notice period to align with procurement calendar requirements and maintain flexibility to evaluate performance near the end of each term.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Missing the 60-day notice window by even a few days automatically commits Customer to one additional full year of fees. In a SaaS context with annual renewals, 60 days is approximately 16% of the renewal period — far exceeding industry norms of 30–45 days. This poses concrete calendar risk for procurement teams managing multiple renewals.
accepted_findings.position: 30-day non-renewal notice
accepted_findings.fallback: 45-day non-renewal notice
accepted_findings.suggested_text: unless either Party provides written notice of non-renewal to the other Party at least thirty (30) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term

accepted_findings.source_text: for thirty (30) days following termination, Lattice will, upon Customer's written request and at Customer's expense, make Customer Data available for download in a commercially reasonable format
accepted_findings.proposed_text: for sixty (60) days following termination, Lattice will, upon Customer's written request and at no additional charge to Customer, make Customer Data available for download in a commercially reasonable format, which shall include CSV, JSON, or API access
accepted_findings.external_comment: Customer Data is Customer's property and should be retrievable without additional cost following termination. A 30-day window may not provide sufficient time to identify, retrieve, and validate large datasets. We request a 60-day retrieval window at no charge and clarification that standard structured-data formats are included.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Requiring Customer to pay for retrieval of its own data post-termination imposes an undefined cost that could range from nominal to substantial depending on data volume. If the data is large (e.g., years of telemetry), the cost could reach tens of thousands of dollars, effectively holding Customer Data hostage. Additionally, 30 days is tight for coordinating retrieval, validation, and migration to a replacement system.
accepted_findings.position: 60-day retrieval window at no charge; formats include CSV, JSON, and API access
accepted_findings.fallback: 45-day retrieval window at no charge for up to one full export; reasonable fees for additional exports or formats
accepted_findings.suggested_text: for sixty (60) days following termination, Lattice will, upon Customer's written request and at no additional charge to Customer, make Customer Data available for download in a commercially reasonable format, which shall include CSV, JSON, or API access

accepted_findings.proposed_text: 3.6 Disputed Invoices. Customer may withhold payment of any specific disputed amount and such withholding shall not constitute a breach or trigger late fees or suspension, provided that Customer notifies Lattice in writing of the basis for the dispute within fifteen (15) business days of the invoice date. Customer shall pay all undisputed amounts in accordance with the payment terms in Section 3.2. The Parties will work in good faith to resolve any disputed amounts within thirty (30) days of Customer's dispute notice.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We would like to add a provision clarifying Customer's ability to withhold specific disputed amounts while paying undisputed portions on the standard schedule, which is common in commercial SaaS agreements and avoids suspension or late-fee disputes over good-faith billing disagreements.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without a dispute-withholding mechanism, Customer risks triggering suspension or late fees when disputing overbillings, scope creep, or billing errors, and must pay first and seek refund later. This shifts leverage inappropriately to Vendor in billing disputes on a multi-year, potentially six-figure subscription.
accepted_findings.position: Customer may withhold specific disputed amounts with 15-day written notice; undisputed amounts paid on standard terms; no late fees or suspension during good-faith dispute resolution; 30-day resolution target.
accepted_findings.fallback: Customer may withhold disputed amounts with 10-day notice; 45-day resolution target; disputes above $10K threshold only.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: 3.6 Disputed Invoices. Customer may withhold payment of any specific disputed amount and such withholding shall not constitute a breach or trigger late fees or suspension, provided that Customer notifies Lattice in writing of the basis for the dispute within fifteen (15) business days of the invoice date. Customer shall pay all undisputed amounts in accordance with the payment terms in Section 3.2. The Parties will work in good faith to resolve any disputed amounts within thirty (30) days of Customer's dispute notice.

accepted_findings.source_text: Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term. Any such increase will take effect at the start of the next Renewal Term.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by no more than five percent (5%) per year, by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term. Any such increase will take effect at the start of the next Renewal Term. Increases exceeding 5% per year require mutual agreement.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We would like to cap annual price increases at 5% per year to provide budget predictability, with any increases above that threshold subject to negotiation. This protects both parties from unilateral escalation beyond inflationary norms.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Uncapped unilateral price escalation on an auto-renewing subscription creates unbounded budget exposure over a 3–5 year relationship. On a $200K annual subscription, Vendor could unilaterally impose $50K increases in year three, forcing Customer to either absorb unexpected cost or terminate mid-relationship. A 5% cap aligns escalation with inflationary norms while preserving Vendor's ability to adjust for cost increases.
accepted_findings.position: Annual price increases capped at 5% per year; increases above 5% require mutual written agreement.
accepted_findings.fallback: Annual price increases capped at 7% per year, or at CPI-U plus 2%, whichever is greater.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by no more than five percent (5%) per year, by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term. Any such increase will take effect at the start of the next Renewal Term. Increases exceeding 5% per year require mutual agreement.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Customer shall be named as an additional insured on Lattice's errors and omissions / professional liability insurance policy, but only with respect to claims arising out of or relating to the Subscription Services and Professional Services provided to Customer under this Agreement.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Customer should be named as additional insured on the E&O policy to ensure direct access to coverage for claims arising from the services. This is consistent with the structure of Section 8.3, which provides Customer with indemnification rights for infringement claims, and ensures the insurance coverage follows that indemnity obligation.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without additional-insured status, Customer must pursue indemnification from Lattice in the event of a covered claim, creating collection risk if Lattice becomes insolvent or disputes indemnity obligations. Additional-insured status provides Customer with direct access to the E&O policy proceeds, reducing Customer's practical risk in the event of an IP infringement or professional-services failure claim.
accepted_findings.position: Customer named as additional insured on E&O policy with respect to claims arising from services provided under this Agreement.
accepted_findings.fallback: Certificate of insurance to confirm AI status delivered annually; Lattice to obtain AI endorsement if not standard in its policy form.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Customer shall be named as an additional insured on Lattice's errors and omissions / professional liability insurance policy, but only with respect to claims arising out of or relating to the Subscription Services and Professional Services provided to Customer under this Agreement.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Lattice's obligation to maintain the insurance coverage required under Section 9.2 shall survive termination or expiration of this Agreement for a period of three (3) years, or Lattice shall obtain extended reporting period (tail) coverage for the same period.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Insurance coverage should survive termination to protect against claims arising from services performed during the term but asserted after termination. A three-year tail is consistent with typical statutes of limitations for contract and negligence claims.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Section 9.2 requires coverage 'during the Subscription Term' only. If Lattice's policy lapses on termination and a claim is later asserted, the insurance that would have backstopped Lattice's indemnity no longer exists, leaving Customer to collect directly from Lattice. A three-year tail ensures the insurance backstop remains available for the period during which post-termination claims are most likely.
accepted_findings.position: Three-year post-termination survival of insurance obligations, or tail coverage for three years.
accepted_findings.fallback: Two-year survival; or Lattice to maintain claims-made coverage with retroactive date covering the entire Subscription Term.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Lattice's obligation to maintain the insurance coverage required under Section 9.2 shall survive termination or expiration of this Agreement for a period of three (3) years, or Lattice shall obtain extended reporting period (tail) coverage for the same period.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Lattice shall require its insurers to provide Customer with at least thirty (30) days' prior written notice of any cancellation, material reduction in coverage, or non-renewal of the policies required under Section 9.2.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Customer should receive advance notice if the required insurance coverage lapses or is materially reduced, to allow Customer to assess the ongoing risk and consider alternate protections.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If Lattice's insurance lapses mid-term without Customer's knowledge, Customer loses the backstop coverage for indemnity and liability claims. Thirty days' notice allows Customer to require proof of replacement coverage or consider termination under Section 4.3 if the lapse constitutes a material breach.
accepted_findings.position: Thirty days' prior written notice to Customer of any cancellation, material reduction, or non-renewal of required policies.
accepted_findings.fallback: Lattice to notify Customer within ten days of receiving notice from its insurer of cancellation or non-renewal.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Lattice shall require its insurers to provide Customer with at least thirty (30) days' prior written notice of any cancellation, material reduction in coverage, or non-renewal of the policies required under Section 9.2.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 7.4: 'The obligations of confidentiality set forth in this Section 7 will continue during the Subscription Term and for a period of three (3) years thereafter, except that with respect to trade secrets such obligations will continue for as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret under applicable law.'
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Section 7.4: 'The obligations of confidentiality set forth in this Section 7 will continue during the Subscription Term and for a period of five (5) years thereafter, except that with respect to trade secrets such obligations will continue for as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret under applicable law.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: The profile requires a minimum 5-year confidentiality tail for non-trade-secret confidential information. The current 3-year tail in Section 7.4 contradicts the profile's explicit requirement and creates an internal inconsistency with Customer's data-protection posture. While no specialist flagged this directly, it must be corrected to maintain coherence with Customer's stated confidentiality requirements.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: The 3-year confidentiality tail falls short of the profile's 5-year minimum requirement. In a SaaS context where Lattice will have multi-year access to Customer's operational data, competitive information, and business intelligence, a 3-year tail exposes Customer Data and business information to disclosure within a period where it retains significant competitive value. This creates concrete harm: Lattice could disclose Customer's confidential pricing, deployment patterns, or integration details to competitors as early as year four of a five-year relationship. The profile explicitly states: 'Mutual; minimum 5-year tail for non-trade-secret confidential information' with rationale '3-year tails are short relative to the lifecycle of competitive information. 5 years is our floor.'
accepted_findings.position: Confidentiality obligations survive for five (5) years post-termination for non-trade-secret information.
accepted_findings.fallback: Four (4) year tail with express carve-out stating that Customer Data remains confidential for five years regardless.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Section 7.4: 'The obligations of confidentiality set forth in this Section 7 will continue during the Subscription Term and for a period of five (5) years thereafter, except that with respect to trade secrets such obligations will continue for as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret under applicable law.'

accepted_findings.source_text: Cross-section: Section 2.3 final sentence ('Customer's sole remedy in the event of repeated failures shall be the right to terminate the affected Subscription Service in accordance with Section 4.3') + accepted finding termination-remedies-analyst-004 (which proposes early termination without remainder-of-term fees for 'three consecutive months of SLA failures').
accepted_findings.external_comment: Section 2.3 states that Customer's sole remedy for repeated SLA failures is termination 'in accordance with Section 4.3,' which currently requires a 60-day cure period for vendor breach and imposes full remainder-of-term fees per Section 4.4(b) unless the termination is 'for Lattice's uncured material breach.' The accepted finding termination-remedies-analyst-004 proposes waiving remainder-of-term fees for termination following 'three consecutive months of SLA failures,' but does not clarify whether such SLA failures constitute 'material breach' under Section 4.3 or whether the 60-day cure period applies. Without coordination, the edited Section 4.4(b) creates an SLA-failure exit path that may still trigger a 60-day cure obligation, negating the intended rapid-exit remedy. We recommend either: (a) adding express language to Section 4.3 that termination for three consecutive months of SLA failures does not require a cure period, or (b) annotating Section 2.3 to clarify that the 'right to terminate' for repeated SLA failures is an immediate right not subject to Section 4.3's cure procedures.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without clarification, Customer may invoke the SLA-failure termination right from the edited Section 4.4(b), only to have Lattice assert that the termination is still subject to the 60-day cure period from Section 4.3 because SLA failures may not rise to 'material breach' or because the cross-reference in Section 2.3 incorporates the cure procedure. This creates ambiguity that will lead to dispute at the worst possible time — when Customer is attempting to exit a failing service. The combination of (1) sole-and-exclusive-remedy language in Section 2.3, (2) the 60-day vendor cure period in Section 4.3, and (3) the SLA-failure carve-out in the edited Section 4.4(b) must be explicitly reconciled to provide Customer with the intended rapid-exit path.
accepted_findings.position: Clarify that termination for three consecutive months of SLA failures (per the edited Section 4.4(b)) is an immediate termination right not subject to the 60-day cure period in Section 4.3, or amend Section 4.3 to add 'or Lattice's failure to meet SLA commitments for three consecutive months' as a separate termination ground with no cure period.
accepted_findings.fallback: Annotate the interaction and flag for manual resolution during redline review.

accepted_findings.source_text: Cross-section: Section 8.6 liability cap ('12 months of Fees paid or payable') + accepted finding risk-allocation-analyst-001 (proposing Security Incident super-cap of '3x Fees paid or payable in the 12 months immediately preceding the Security Incident') + Section 5.3 (Security Incident notification within 72 hours).
accepted_findings.external_comment: The accepted super-cap for Security Incidents uses the formula '3x Fees paid or payable in the 12 months immediately preceding the Security Incident,' which differs from the general cap formula 'Fees paid or payable... in the 12 months immediately preceding the event giving rise to the liability.' In most cases these are identical, but they may diverge if: (a) the Security Incident occurs but liability does not crystallize until later (e.g., regulatory penalty assessed 18 months after the breach), or (b) Customer has scaled up or down significantly between the breach date and the liability event. We recommend clarifying whether 'immediately preceding the Security Incident' means the breach discovery date per Section 5.3, the date Lattice becomes aware of unauthorized access, or the date liability is asserted. This is a technical ambiguity unlikely to create material dispute but should be noted for clean drafting.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: In a scenario where a Security Incident occurs in month 10 of a subscription, is discovered in month 12, and regulatory penalties are assessed in month 18, the 'immediately preceding' language could reference three different 12-month windows depending on interpretation. If Customer has scaled up fees significantly in the interim, the difference could be tens or hundreds of thousands of dollars in cap headroom. This is a lower-severity coherence issue but merits annotation for reviewer awareness.
accepted_findings.position: Clarify that 'immediately preceding the Security Incident' means the date of breach discovery as defined in Section 5.3, or align the Security Incident super-cap formula with the general cap formula to use 'immediately preceding the event giving rise to the liability.'
accepted_findings.fallback: Leave as-is and rely on general contract-interpretation principles; note in external comment for reviewer.

accepted_findings.source_text: Cross-section: Section 10.5 entire-agreement clause ('This Agreement, together with all Order Forms, SOWs, and any addenda or exhibits attached hereto or referenced herein, constitutes the entire agreement') + accepted finding critical-issues-auditor-003 (proposing new Section 9.1(a) with subcontractor notice and approval rights) + Section 5.4 (incorporation of Data Processing Addendum by reference).
accepted_findings.external_comment: The entire-agreement clause in Section 10.5 states the Agreement 'together with all Order Forms, SOWs, and any addenda or exhibits' constitutes the entire agreement. The accepted finding critical-issues-auditor-003 proposes adding Section 9.1(a), which grants Customer the right to 'a list of material subcontractors... upon Customer's written request' and requires '30 days' prior written notice of any change.' If this list is provided as a separate document (e.g., in an email or attachment not formally incorporated as an exhibit), it may fall outside the four corners of the 'entire agreement' and create ambiguity about its enforceability. Similarly, Section 5.4 incorporates the Data Processing Addendum 'by reference,' but if the DPA is not attached as an exhibit or formally executed, its status may be ambiguous. We recommend clarifying that any subcontractor list provided under the amended Section 9.1(a) is deemed part of the Agreement or is incorporated by reference, and ensuring the DPA is either attached as Exhibit B or expressly incorporated by reference in Section 10.5.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If the subcontractor list or DPA is provided separately and not formally incorporated, Lattice could argue that Customer's objection to a subcontractor change or invocation of DPA obligations is unenforceable because the list/DPA is not part of the 'entire agreement.' This is a technical contract-formation issue that creates enforceability risk for the subcontractor-governance and data-protection frameworks Customer is negotiating for. Best practice is to ensure all governing documents are either attached as exhibits or incorporated by reference in the entire-agreement clause.
accepted_findings.position: Amend Section 10.5 to add 'and the Data Processing Addendum and any subcontractor lists provided under Section 9.1(a)' to the list of documents constituting the entire agreement, or require such documents to be attached as numbered exhibits.
accepted_findings.fallback: Add a sentence to the amended Section 9.1(a) stating 'Any subcontractor list provided hereunder shall be deemed incorporated into this Agreement by reference.'

rejected_findings.source_text: Cross-section of §8.6 liability cap carve-out '(C) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY)' (uncapped) + proposed Customer indemnity in risk-allocation-analyst-003 (if accepted) remaining subject to the cap unless separately carved out.
rejected_findings.external_comment: Section 8.3 provides Lattice IP indemnity to Customer, carved out from the liability cap per Section 8.6(C). If mutual indemnification for IP and confidentiality is implemented as proposed, the parties should clarify whether both parties' indemnity obligations are uncapped to maintain symmetry. If Customer's indemnity remains capped while Lattice's is uncapped, Customer faces uncapped liability for third-party IP claims arising from Customer Data (potentially multi-million-dollar exposure), while Lattice's exposure for non-IP claims is capped at 1x fees. This asymmetry contradicts the requirement for indemnity symmetry.
rejected_findings.materiality_rationale: The combination creates material asymmetric exposure: uncapped vendor indemnity + capped customer indemnity violates the playbook's symmetry requirement. On agreements where Customer may upload third-party-licensed content, uncapped Customer IP indemnity exposure could reach seven figures if a major IP holder asserts infringement, while Lattice's capped liability for service failures or data loss remains at 1x fees. This imbalance is material and negotiable.
rejected_findings.position: If mutual indemnity is implemented, both parties' IP indemnity obligations must be uncapped (carved out from §8.6) to maintain symmetry. Alternatively, both parties' IP indemnity obligations are subject to a symmetric super-cap (e.g., 3x fees or insurance limits).
rejected_findings.fallback: Customer IP indemnity capped at 2x trailing fees; Lattice IP indemnity uncapped. Asymmetry justified by differential risk profile.
rejected_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per §10.1). Indemnity provisions are enforceable; asymmetry in cap treatment does not affect enforceability but contradicts Customer's playbook and creates disproportionate exposure.
rejected_findings.rejection_reason: LLM escalation: edit moves contract against client in role.

rejected_findings.source_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net sixty (60) days from the date of invoice.
rejected_findings.proposed_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.
rejected_findings.external_comment: We would like to align payment terms with our standard Net-30 cycle, which is consistent with market practice for SaaS subscriptions of this type and size.
rejected_findings.materiality_rationale: Net-60 extends working capital exposure by 30 days beyond Customer's AP cycle, creating payment-tracking friction and increasing float cost on annual prepayments. On a $150K+ annual subscription, the 30-day delta represents material cash-flow inefficiency.
rejected_findings.fallback: Net-45 from date of invoice if Vendor offers a 2% early-payment discount or if the subscription exceeds $500K annually.
rejected_findings.suggested_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.
rejected_findings.rejection_reason: Proposed Net 30 shortens payment timing from Net 60; Customer-side reviews must not decrease Net-term days.

auditor_findings.source_text: Cross-section of §4.4(b) early-termination fee obligation ('Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term') + §2.3 sole-remedy for SLA failures (service credits only, with termination right for 'repeated failures') + §4.3 asymmetric cure periods (30-day customer cure vs 60-day vendor cure).
auditor_findings.external_comment: The specialists correctly identified that §4.4(b) creates remainder-of-term fee liability (termination-remedies-analyst-004, marked blocker), that §2.3 limits SLA remedies to credits (performance-obligations-analyst-001, marked major), and that §4.3 cure periods are asymmetric (termination-remedies-analyst-002, marked blocker). However, no specialist caught the cross-section trap: Customer facing repeated material SLA failures cannot cure Lattice's performance within the 60-day window (SLA failures are ongoing service defects, not discrete curable breaches), cannot terminate for cause without triggering full remainder-of-term fees (because §2.3 frames SLA failures as giving rise only to credits and a separate termination right that likely does not qualify as 'Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3'), and thus is economically locked into a non-performing service for the duration of a multi-year term. On a $300K annual subscription with 18 months remaining, Customer owes $450K to exit a service that may be delivering <90% uptime. This is an existential economic trap that emerges only when all three provisions are read together.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: Individual specialists correctly flagged components: termination-remedies-analyst-004 marked the early-termination fee as blocker-severity and proposed waiving fees for 'repeated SLA failures.' Performance-obligations-analyst-001 marked the sole-remedy framing for SLA failures as major-severity and noted internal contradiction with the termination right. Termination-remedies-analyst-002 marked asymmetric cure as blocker. However, no specialist synthesized these into the cross-section hazard: the interaction of sole-remedy language + termination-for-repeated-failures + early-termination-fee-unless-uncured-breach creates ambiguity over whether the SLA-failure termination right in §2.3 qualifies as termination 'for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3' (which would avoid fees under §4.4(b)). If it does not—and the sole-remedy language in §2.3 suggests it is a separate contractual right, not a breach remedy—then Customer must pay full remainder fees even when terminating due to Lattice's sustained service failures. This is an exit trap that individual specialists, each operating within their domain silo, structurally could not see. The financial exposure (potentially hundreds of thousands of dollars in fees for zero service value over the remaining term) combined with the operational harm of being unable to exit a failing service makes this existential for Customer.
auditor_findings.position: Clarify that termination under §2.3 for repeated SLA failures constitutes termination for Lattice's material breach and does not trigger early-termination fees under §4.4(b). Alternatively, cap early-termination fees at 3 months of subscription fees when Customer terminates due to three consecutive months of SLA failures.
auditor_findings.fallback: Add carve-out to §4.4(b): 'Customer shall not owe early-termination fees if Customer terminates following material SLA failures as defined in Exhibit A (specifically, failure to meet uptime SLA for three consecutive months or failure to meet response-time SLA in more than 20% of incidents over any rolling 90-day period).'
auditor_findings.walkaway: Customer cannot accept a contract structure where sustained vendor service failures (SLA breaches) trap Customer into paying hundreds of thousands of dollars in fees for a non-performing service with no economically viable exit. This issue must be resolved before execution.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per §10.1). Delaware courts construe sole-remedy and early-termination-fee provisions strictly and may void early-termination fees as unenforceable penalties if they are not reasonably related to anticipated harm. However, litigation to establish unenforceability is costly and uncertain; contractual clarity is required.

auditor_findings.source_text: Cross-section of §8.6 liability cap carve-out '(C) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY)' (uncapped) + proposed Customer indemnity in risk-allocation-analyst-003 (if accepted) remaining subject to the cap unless separately carved out.
auditor_findings.external_comment: Risk-allocation-analyst-003 proposed adding mutual Customer indemnity for IP infringement and confidentiality breach (marked moderate-severity, not existential). Risk-allocation-analyst-005 noted in a tier-2 finding that if Customer indemnity is added, the parties should clarify whether it is capped or uncapped, given that Lattice IP indemnity is uncapped per §8.6(C). However, neither specialist elevated this to tier 1 or marked it as creating asymmetric exposure that contradicts the Profile's requirement for indemnity symmetry. The cross-section hazard is: if the parties accept mutual indemnity (which Profile requires) but leave Customer's indemnity subject to the cap while Lattice's remains uncapped, Customer faces uncapped liability for third-party IP claims arising from Customer Data (potentially multi-million-dollar exposure if Customer uploads infringing content at scale), while Lattice's exposure for non-IP claims is capped at 1x fees (potentially $100K–$500K). This asymmetry directly contradicts playbook.indemnification.position, which requires symmetry on indemnities 'of equal weight.'
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: Risk-allocation-analyst-003 correctly identified the absence of mutual indemnity and proposed adding Customer IP indemnity, marking it moderate-severity and playbook-applicable. Risk-allocation-analyst-005 flagged the cap-interaction ambiguity as a tier-2 clarity issue. However, neither specialist recognized that the combination creates material asymmetric exposure: uncapped vendor indemnity + capped customer indemnity = Profile violation and disproportionate risk allocation. Individual specialists look only at their domain; this hazard requires seeing two liability-allocation provisions simultaneously and recognizing that their interaction contradicts the playbook's symmetry requirement. On a mid-market SaaS deal where Customer may upload third-party-licensed content (e.g., customer logos, marketing collateral, integration data from other vendors), uncapped Customer IP indemnity exposure could reach seven figures if a major IP holder asserts infringement, while Lattice's capped liability for service failures, data loss, or non-IP harms remains capped at 1x fees. This imbalance is material and negotiable.
auditor_findings.position: If mutual indemnity is implemented per risk-allocation-analyst-003, both parties' IP indemnity obligations must be uncapped (carved out from §8.6) to maintain symmetry. Alternatively, both parties' IP indemnity obligations are subject to a symmetric super-cap (e.g., 3x fees or insurance limits).
auditor_findings.fallback: Customer IP indemnity capped at 2x trailing fees; Lattice IP indemnity uncapped. Asymmetry justified by differential risk profile (Vendor controls platform code; Customer controls uploaded data).
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per §10.1). Indemnity provisions are enforceable; asymmetry in cap treatment does not affect enforceability but contradicts Customer's playbook and creates disproportionate exposure.

auditor_findings.proposed_text: Add new Section 9.1(a): 'Subcontractor Obligations. Lattice shall require all subcontractors that process Customer Data or provide material components of the Subscription Services to comply with obligations no less protective than those imposed on Lattice under this Agreement, including without limitation the confidentiality obligations in Section 7, the data security obligations in Section 5.2, and the insurance requirements in Section 9.2. Lattice shall remain fully liable for the acts and omissions of its subcontractors as if they were Lattice's own acts and omissions. Upon Customer's written request, Lattice shall provide Customer with a list of material subcontractors (defined as subcontractors processing Customer Data or providing more than 25% of the Subscription Services functionality) and shall provide Customer with thirty (30) days' prior written notice of any change to such material subcontractors, during which period Customer may object on reasonable grounds related to data security, compliance, or confidentiality. If Customer objects and the parties cannot resolve the objection within fifteen (15) days, Customer may terminate the affected Subscription Service without penalty.'
auditor_findings.external_comment: Section 9.1 permits Lattice to 'engage third parties to perform any of its obligations under this Agreement, including without limitation hosting, content delivery, support, and processing of Customer Data,' and states that 'Lattice will remain responsible for the performance of any such subcontractors and for compliance by such subcontractors with the applicable terms of this Agreement.' However, §9.1 does not: (a) require Lattice to impose contractual flowdown obligations on subcontractors (leaving Lattice 'responsible' but with no contractual leverage over subcontractors to enforce that responsibility); (b) provide Customer with notice or approval rights for subcontractors processing Customer Data; or (c) provide Customer with a remedy if a subcontractor's data-handling practices create compliance or security risk for Customer. Customer's playbook requires 'notice + customer approval for material subcontractors (defined as those processing customer data or providing >25% of services)' with 'vendor flowdown of obligations.' The current provision fails to meet this standard.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: No specialist flagged subcontracting governance as a standalone issue. Commercial-terms-analyst's coverage_pass shows subcontracting was not on their checklist. Risk-allocation-analyst's checklist does not include subcontracting (that would typically fall under operational/vendor-management issues, not liability allocation). Performance-obligations-analyst did not address subcontracting in their coverage_pass (not a performance-specification issue). Termination-remedies-analyst similarly would not cover this. Thus, no specialist had domain responsibility for subcontracting rights, and the item fell into a coverage gap. This is a material omission because: (1) Profile explicitly requires notice + approval for material subcontractors and flowdown of obligations (playbook.subcontracting.position and .rationale cite data-handling supply-chain visibility for compliance and security reviews); (2) in a SaaS context, subcontractors (hosting providers, CDN providers, support vendors) routinely have direct access to Customer Data, and a subcontractor's security failure or non-compliance can create direct liability for Customer under data-breach notification laws, GDPR, or industry-specific regulations; (3) without notice or approval rights, Customer has no visibility into which third parties hold Customer Data and no ability to vet them or terminate if a high-risk subcontractor is introduced. On a subscription processing sensitive operational data, lack of subcontractor governance is a significant compliance and security gap.
auditor_findings.position: Notice required + Customer approval for material subcontractors (defined as those processing Customer Data or providing >25% of services). Vendor must impose flowdown of confidentiality, data-security, and insurance obligations on all subcontractors. Customer may object to subcontractor changes on reasonable data-security or compliance grounds, with termination right if objection cannot be resolved.
auditor_findings.fallback: Notice required for material subcontractors with 15-day objection window; no approval required but Customer may terminate without penalty if new subcontractor creates material compliance or security risk. Flowdown obligations required.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per §10.1). Subcontracting without flowdown obligations or customer notice is common in vendor-favorable SaaS agreements but creates compliance and security risk for customers in regulated industries or handling sensitive data.
auditor_findings.suggested_text: Add new Section 9.1(a): 'Subcontractor Obligations. Lattice shall require all subcontractors that process Customer Data or provide material components of the Subscription Services to comply with obligations no less protective than those imposed on Lattice under this Agreement, including without limitation the confidentiality obligations in Section 7, the data security obligations in Section 5.2, and the insurance requirements in Section 9.2. Lattice shall remain fully liable for the acts and omissions of its subcontractors as if they were Lattice's own acts and omissions. Upon Customer's written request, Lattice shall provide Customer with a list of material subcontractors (defined as subcontractors processing Customer Data or providing more than 25% of the Subscription Services functionality) and shall provide Customer with thirty (30) days' prior written notice of any change to such material subcontractors, during which period Customer may object on reasonable grounds related to data security, compliance, or confidentiality. If Customer objects and the parties cannot resolve the objection within fifteen (15) days, Customer may terminate the affected Subscription Service without penalty.'

coherence_findings.source_text: Section 7.4: 'The obligations of confidentiality set forth in this Section 7 will continue during the Subscription Term and for a period of three (3) years thereafter, except that with respect to trade secrets such obligations will continue for as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret under applicable law.'
coherence_findings.proposed_text: Section 7.4: 'The obligations of confidentiality set forth in this Section 7 will continue during the Subscription Term and for a period of five (5) years thereafter, except that with respect to trade secrets such obligations will continue for as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret under applicable law.'
coherence_findings.external_comment: The profile requires a minimum 5-year confidentiality tail for non-trade-secret confidential information. The current 3-year tail in Section 7.4 contradicts the profile's explicit requirement and creates an internal inconsistency with Customer's data-protection posture. While no specialist flagged this directly, it must be corrected to maintain coherence with Customer's stated confidentiality requirements.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: The 3-year confidentiality tail falls short of the profile's 5-year minimum requirement. In a SaaS context where Lattice will have multi-year access to Customer's operational data, competitive information, and business intelligence, a 3-year tail exposes Customer Data and business information to disclosure within a period where it retains significant competitive value. This creates concrete harm: Lattice could disclose Customer's confidential pricing, deployment patterns, or integration details to competitors as early as year four of a five-year relationship. The profile explicitly states: 'Mutual; minimum 5-year tail for non-trade-secret confidential information' with rationale '3-year tails are short relative to the lifecycle of competitive information. 5 years is our floor.'
coherence_findings.position: Confidentiality obligations survive for five (5) years post-termination for non-trade-secret information.
coherence_findings.fallback: Four (4) year tail with express carve-out stating that Customer Data remains confidential for five years regardless.
coherence_findings.suggested_text: Section 7.4: 'The obligations of confidentiality set forth in this Section 7 will continue during the Subscription Term and for a period of five (5) years thereafter, except that with respect to trade secrets such obligations will continue for as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret under applicable law.'

coherence_findings.source_text: Cross-section: Section 2.3 final sentence ('Customer's sole remedy in the event of repeated failures shall be the right to terminate the affected Subscription Service in accordance with Section 4.3') + accepted finding termination-remedies-analyst-004 (which proposes early termination without remainder-of-term fees for 'three consecutive months of SLA failures').

We recommend either: (a) adding express language to Section 4.3 that termination for three consecutive months of SLA failures does not require a cure period, or (b) annotating Section 2.3 to clarify that the 'right to terminate' for repeated SLA failures is an immediate right not subject to Section 4.3's cure procedures.

This creates ambiguity that will lead to dispute at the worst possible time — when Customer is attempting to exit a failing service. The combination of (1) sole-and-exclusive-remedy language in Section 2.3, (2) the 60-day vendor cure period in Section 4.3, and (3) the SLA-failure carve-out in the edited Section 4.4(b) must be explicitly reconciled to provide Customer with the intended rapid-exit path.
